speaker: interviewer
text: Edith, we've got to the point where war broke out and you were unable to go to school anymore. Do you want to tell us a little bit more about what life was like then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, life was quite difficult. Well, people tried to emigrate, but again, that was very difficult. There was a rumor that you can get a visa to A, so people queued at that consulate. They obtained the visa but they couldn't get the transit visas because you had to go by train through other countries, which refused to give you transit visas. Or you got a transit visa and then the final visa already expired. It was a very difficult time. Also people who wanted to go to America or somewhere by boat-- well, it was very difficult to secure a birth. And it was a state of panic everywhere. People just trying to escape, not knowing what, but they knew things were going to be bad. [SIGHS] And the people who couldn't escape-- they just lived as well as they could and tried to exist from day to day. And then the worst thing was when the transport started in '41. Prior to that, they send Polish-- former Polish citizens, people who were born in Poland and emigrated to Czechoslovakia after World War I-- they were sent back to Poland I think much earlier, either in '39 or '40. But somehow we didn't-- it didn't touch us so much. Because we were, after all, Czech Jews and this couldn't happen to us. And then the deportation started and we-- 5,000 people were sent to Lodz and to the Lodz ghetto. And then, and in November '41, Theresienstadt was founded. And the transport started to go to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were hopeful that it will be the camp for Czech Jews and that we stay there for the duration of the war. And that-- in our naivete, we thought when the war finishes, we'll just go back home and things will be as before. But of course, it was far cry from that. And your friends sort of disappeared. Because we weren't allowed to have telephones anymore. We weren't allowed to have radios. So you had telephones before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't have a telephone. But who-- whoever had telephone--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --they weren't allowed to have one. You had to hand in your radio. You had to hand in your jewelry, the bank books. You had to-- then, when they started the Russian campaign, you had to hand in your warm clothing and your skis, the ski equipment. And people weren't allowed to have pets. And there were so many regulations. You were hamstrung right through. And so people-- as I said, people just didn't appear in the usual places. And you knew they had been taken on a transport. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea where they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew it was to Theresienstadt--

speaker: interviewer
text: Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: --at that stage. But we didn't know-- what we didn't know, that there were transports from Theresienstadt going east.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that they, sort of every week or so, the transport was always 1,000. And again, there were the same number of people going out of Theresienstadt, which we had no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were allowed to take a suitcase and as much as we can carry. And they sort of prescribed what should be in the suitcase, that you should take practical things and think of winter clothes. And we had to have a aluminum or enamel bowl for washing in. And we had to have a military-- one of those-- I don't know what they're called, the mess tins or whatever, and a tin cup, and tin utensils for eating, and I don't know what else.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had-- oh, yes. an empty-- [SIGHS] an empty-- for-- would I call it? To put straw in to sleep on? What-- what would you call it? A-- not a mattress, really, just a bag to put straw in so that you can sleep on it. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so forth, and so forth. But we never saw the-- the previous transports still got the suitcases. Ours was the first one who didn't get the suitcases. So [SIGHS] we only had what we carried. And it was the height of summer. And I remember I had a summer frock over which I had a skirt, a winter skirt, and a blouse, and a sweater, and a jacket, and a raincoat, and a winter jacket.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had all the clothes on.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the clothes on. My mother always lamented that she only could put on one pair of shoes and not two. [LAUGHS] I think we even had galoshes over the shoes, just to take as much as possible. And we were allowed to take five pounds of food, I think, or food for five days. That's right-- food for five days. And when we got to the rail head, we had to walk-- it was about a three-hour walk to the camp. And lots of people couldn't, of course, carry their luggage. So there were people with carts and just said, well, if you can't carry the luggage, just put it on the cart. And that was the last--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you never saw that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never saw. And when we packed the rucksacks, I was a Girl Guard for many years. And I knew how much I could carry. And my mother put more and more. And I said, look, you can't carry it. Mom said, oh, yes I can. I said, no, you can't. Well, she couldn't. And she put it on the cart. And she never got her rucksack. So we actually only had the one--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yours.

speaker: interviewee
text: --my rucksack between [LAUGHS] the two of us. And we had two bags with food. And when we got into the ghetto, they-- people weren't allowed to go on the streets. When the transport went in or out people were not allowed on the streets. And all of a sudden, a man darts out of a doorway, snatches my bag, and runs off. And I recognized him as a family friend. I said to mom, you know, he's been here only three weeks, and what it's done to him. He has already stole my bag. What I didn't realize was that he took it before we get to the control so that we would have it, that he really risked his life doing that. But--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. --I thought he-- at the time, I was shattered. I thought he stole it. And then we had to go-- then the German gendarmerie checked us through, whether we didn't swindle anything in. And they checked the luggage for money, or jewelry, and so forth. And whatever they liked, they still took out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And eventually, you were told where to go. Sort of, there were the old people's-- the people's houses, which were empty, and shops and so forth, and nothing but bare boards. And we were directed to a former shop. So when you came late, the people who were there for a while, they got the bits along the wall, where they could perhaps put a nail into the wall. And when you came late, you were in the middle where everybody walked over you. And they had to go to the loo at nighttime, because everybody had dysentery. So of course, we were in the middle of the room. And I remember like today, it was near the steps to the shop. And the boards were worn. And they were extremely uneven. They were like a washboard. [LAUGHS] And I could never find a spot to lie to, because just to lie on the-- on the floorboards was highly uncomfortable. So I've done it many times as a scout, but it was difficult. And a friend came and said to us, we should find some protected occupation and told us what would be advisable. And my mother got a job as a nursing sister for old people because nobody wanted to do it. The old people all suffered from dysentery, and lice, and God knows what. And there were no sanitary means at all. And of course, the toilets were highly inadequate and everything was highly inadequate.

speaker: interviewee
text: So to get people do that, they decided to make it a protected occupation. So my mother did that. And because I did this graphic course, I got a job in the-- the Germans had a-- in the [GERMAN], they devoted [BACKGROUND NOISE] the upper floor to have people who were artists, painters, and so forth, to draw pictures. And then they printed them. And then they colored them. We had to color them in with aniline paints. And they sold those pictures as bookmarks or calendars and whatnot. So we had a quota to fill.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was extremely lucky-- when we arrived, there was-- that was called a [GERMAN], the [GERMAN], when you arrived at the barracks and they processed you. And all of a sudden, a former pupil of my mother's arrived there. My mother and his mother used to be great friends. And he came in the first transport. And as such, he was protected. Because it was the [GERMAN] that sort of builds the camp and they were protected. And he said, till my mother comes and my fiance, I'll put you on my card and you'll be protected, at least from the next transport. Because what they practicality did-- the transport-- they arrived. And the people who weren't protected, they went straight east. And he says, of course, when my mother and my fiance arrives, well, you'll be off my card. But by that time, you probably already find your way. So he actually saved our life doing that. And so that was the arrival in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And what date was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was the 3rd of August, '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- three days previous to that, we had to assemble in the assembly place in Prague, which was the trade-- former trade fair, which was a huge building. And it housed-- there were 1,000 of us. Sort of the walls had numbers. And I was 461. My mother was 462. So you found your place. And there you-- that was the place where you stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, there were latrines. There were no proper toilets. And it was a dreadful shock for me to--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: --have to go, more or less, in public. [LAUGHS] And it was-- before that, of course, I forgot to mention that we had to register with the Gestapo. As Jews, we had to register and give them all the particulars of bank accounts or whatever, whatever property. And that, of course-- they checked it against the registry of the Jewish community. They had all the names. And then people had to prove that they were Aryan. They had to prove that their grandparents were baptized. And whoever couldn't prove it was considered a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was-- there was no way that you'd get out of the net. Because it was too well organized, too well policed. Right. And when you got to Theresienstadt, how long did it take you to get this job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I got it practically immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And so what sort of hours did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked nine hours a day. And we had a quota of 100 pictures to color in. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: With paint or gouache?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aniline color, which is a transparent color.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Is it like a type of a water color?

speaker: interviewee
text: Water color type of thing. And we were given the original. And then we had to color in the prints.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was there-- was it more technical or sort of quite artistic, in terms of how you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, technical, mainly that you don't go over the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Lines.

speaker: interviewee
text: --lines, et cetera, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just had to have good motor control?

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, the girls who worked there before-- they got the easier designs. They knew what to look for. And the newer ones got the more difficult ones. And if you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or the detailed ones.

speaker: interviewee
text: More detailed. And if you didn't do your quota, well, you went on the next transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [LAUGHS] [SIGHS] it was pretty hard work.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had-- you had to do 100 a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: 100 A day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of size were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I was doing were calendar type of pictures. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I should say about four inches by five inches or something like that. And it would have five, six, seven colors in it. So you did one color first, and then you did the next color, and then the third color, and so forth. So it was quite-- you worried all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, so very stressful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stressful. And out of the window, you could see the mountains, the-- in the distance. And you sort of always thought, why can't I be a bird, be there? Why have I got to be here? And you never got out of the ghetto. It was just so confining-- a small place and more and more people arriving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they started bringing in the special cases from Austria and Germany, people who were-- had medals from-- soldiers from World War I, decorated men, people who were wounded in the war-- wounded-- prominent people.

speaker: interviewee
text: People had to pay. In Germany, they told them it's a spa. And they had to buy their own villa. They had to pay for the privilege to come to Theresienstadt. And when these poor old people arrived, dressed for a spa, can you imagine? And they thought we robbed them. They couldn't understand it. But they said, but I paid for a villa in the-- in the lake promenade. We said, there's no lake. There's no promenade. Oh, how shocking.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was-- it was-- they couldn't adjust. And of course, lots of the old people were dying. Because they got less-- when you were working, you got a bigger ration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The old people got the smallest ration. They couldn't take the food. When I got to Theresienstadt, I couldn't eat for three weeks. It was just so dreadful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Terrible food.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't. And of course, once I got very hungry, I started eating. And then I got the inevitable [SIGHS] dysen-- dysentery, as everybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they give you to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we arrived, the staple was some vegetables, which were half rotten, were half black. And the bread was always green, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Moldy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Moldy. It was summer. And you got a quarter of a long loaf, like the long continental loaf.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You got a quarter of that for four days. And by the time you got it, it was moldy. And then we got some vegetables that were used to feed animals. And we got that. And that was rotten and quite horrible. So you didn't get anything that was in any way edible actually to-- to what you were used to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as children, we then, later on-- about a month later-- a barracks opened for girls between 12 and 15. And I had the possibility to go there, which I did. And we got special children-- extra-- a bun, one bun a week we got extra. So that was already terrific. [LAUGHS] We got a bit extra sustenance. And it was great. I enjoyed being with other girls my age. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So before that, were you with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother and all the old women. And everybody complained, you know. And with the girls, it was a bit better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And were there any girls that you knew from Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were girls I knew, I had met before. So it was-- was great. And the ladies that looked after us-- lots of them were very, very good and very inspired, and [SIGHS] sort of helped you through it. So it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they also Jewish then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nobody-- except for the people who watched us, everybody was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And were there social activities in Theresienstadt, like in the evenings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and more than anywhere else, because the conditions were better than in Poland. And there were concerts. There was opera. There were all sorts-- cabaret. There were talks. There were lectures. I used to go to a concert nearly every day. And I used to sort of have to keep myself from sleeping, because I was enormously tired. But I still went. I always loved music, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- did they have a special place for you to have that? Or it was outdoors, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the beginning, it was secret. They had it on-- in-- in the roof space in the barracks. There was a piano, which only had two legs. They left it behind. The Czechs left it behind in the school. So somebody constructed a thing to keep the piano upright. And there were concerts in the roof space in the old school. And then there were sort of-- the Germans, later, got to know about it. But they tolerated it. And they did not prosecute or do anything. So that was-- it gave us a lot of strength, actually, these activities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You could forget, for a little while, the conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you spend in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, that's amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- after a while, there was an epidemic of various-- there were always epidemics. And first, I got chickenpox, which I already had. But I had it very badly. And then I had hepatitis. And then I got typhoid. So they-- the manufacture-- sort of, that finished for me, where I was, that-- I don't know whether they it carried on after that. And I was about six months in hospital. I couldn't walk. I was very debilitated. And when I got out, I-- eventually my great dream was to get into the agricultural production, because you could steal. And eventually, I managed to get into it in March, the following year, March '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that like farming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, farming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There were huge-- you see, Theresienstadt, having been a military town, there were huge areas for the maneuvers, and for the training of horses, and so forth. So we had to turn them over manually. And we established big-- what you'd call a market garden today-- huge plantations. We had over 11,000 tomato plants, for instance, and thousands of cabbages, and lettuce, and whatever. So we had to tend these things. And we worked very, very hard. In summer, we had to work from sunup to sundown. And it was very hot. And we never got any water to drink. And there was always the possibility-- there was one SS that used to go on his bike so-- on his pushbike-- so we wouldn't hear him. And he'd hide somewhere in the bushes. And if he saw somebody not working properly, he'd pounce on them. So there was always that possibility, so one worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you work in the agricultural--

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the end. I was there--

speaker: interviewer
text: From March--

speaker: interviewee
text: March '43 till May '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your grandmother go with you to Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, grandmother was taken a month before us. And she was-- I was going to say, she was pretty old. She was 72. [LAUGHS] I just checked myself. And she couldn't-- she was pretty feeble. So they just threw her onto a open truck and took her to the assembly point. And she had to do all this on her own. And when we came to Theresienstadt, she was in an old people's barracks. And so we-- we used to go every day and comb her hair, and wash her, so she wouldn't get lice. And then both mom and I were sick, so we couldn't look after her. And she got lice, and they took her to the de-lousing, and that was a very dreadful winter. And what they did-- they took the people's clothes and put it through the disinfection. And then people had to have a shower. And then they chased them out into the yard--

speaker: interviewee
text: With no clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --naked. And of course, she got pneumonia. And fortunately, she died very quickly. I was awfully worried about her when-- when I was sick, personally. I didn't have any news from either my mother or my grandmother. And I used to worry awfully about what's happening [SIGHS] and whether they are alive or what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I came out of hospital, of course, the place I had in my room was gone. The mattress I had was gone. All my things were gone. Because they thought I had died. Nobody knew what happened. So it was a very disappointing time, in a way. Then some-- I got some of my clothes back. Some of it already had gone east, of course, with other people having gone in transport. So whoever was there gave me my things back. But I was sort of shunted from one room to the other when I wasn't-- lots of my friend died in the epidemic. So it was quite--

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS] Traumatic. And especially, I'm sure, being a teenager.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, well, when that--

speaker: interviewer
text: And so when-- did you mother survive the--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother survived. My mother didn't have typhoid. She only had hepatitis, so she survived that. And my grandmother, as I said, had died meanwhile. And my mother worked all the time as-- as a nurse. I sometimes used to bring-- get her lunch for her. You know, we got little tickets. And so I could go and get her lunch. Oh, lunch-- I mean soup. And I'd bring it to her. And I could never understand how she could eat. There was such a stench in the house. It was just so dreadful. I-- I could never understand how she could eat. But she was probably immune. She worked six days a week. They worked 12 hours a day, six days a week. And she looked an old woman. She was-- she was in her early 40s and she liked 70. She looked dreadful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: She worked so hard. And she had to lift the patients to wash them. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quite dreadful. But she did it. She-- because she protected me through her work. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed there until what date? [SIGHS] I think the 9th of May. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: 8th of May, 9th of May-- I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: '45. And did you have any news from the outside world? Were you aware at that time of how the war was progressing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We had no news whatever. All we knew is when transports went to the east.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we could see airplanes overhead. And then they were throwing those little silver for us down. We weren't allowed to look up. And we weren't allowed to collect them. So we knew things were going pretty badly for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, at the end, we saw they were burning documents. We could see bits of paper coming out of the chimneys. And so we knew that they were sort of trying to cover their tracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they German Gestapos in the camp or was it Czech Gestapos?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was German Gestapo and Czech gendarmerie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Czechs were sort of auxiliaries. The Gestapo ruled-- ruled the camp. And towards the end then, on the 20th of April, transports started to come in. The death marches started to come in from the east. People came in on open wagons, on foot. They weren't people. They were just--

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were people who came out of concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of concentration camps and had to walk on--

speaker: interviewer
text: So was that the first time you were aware--

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the death marches. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- there were hardly any men in the camp that-- already, at the end. And I was unloading at the-- meanwhile, they put a spur into the camp. And I was unloading-- trying to unload. And there were French women. And one was crying. She said, my children. They burned my children. They burned my children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I kept saying, oh yeah, yeah. And I thought, well, my French must be poor. Maybe she said something else. And then somebody else was saying it in Yiddish. And [SIGHS] I thought, this is funny. Why should they burn the children? And then one said, they burned my parents. And they gassed my parents. And it took me quite a while to-- to comprehend the whole thing. And once we knew it, we lost all hope that we'll see anybody who's gone east.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the typhus came with these people. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: They brought the germs with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought the lice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And lots of people died, still after the end of the war. And we tried to care for these people. But meanwhile, the Germans have taken anything they could take. And they sent their families westward. And they took-- all the provisions that there were for the camp, they took with them. And they took the livestock. They took just anything. And there was just nothing in the camp to-- to give those people, to help them survive. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So on the last day of the war, we-- [SIGHS] a group of us, we were working out in the fields. And we had glass houses, where we had cucumbers. Well, we didn't. They had. And so we decided we'll go and get the cucumbers, or whatever we can find, any leaves or anything, just so that there is something in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we were trying to harvest these. And on the road, which was next to the garden, the Germans were fleeing. The German columns were fleeing from the north. And the Russian pilots came down and bombed the road. And then they saw us. So they thought it's going to be fun to hit us, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were trying to hide. But they-- in the glass house. But they could see us from-- they shot at us, they-- with the machine guns or whatever. So in the end, we thought there's nothing for it. We've got to make a run. And there was a moat [SIGHS] around the fortifications. And there was a bridge across the moat, which was fairly exposed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we just decided, well, we've got to make a break for it and just run across the bridge. Well, we all managed. There were about 12 of us. We all managed to get back in. But we didn't bring the cucumber or anything because we just left it there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the-- the Russians didn't realize what we were or who we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They just probably thought we were Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, in a day or two, the German-- the Russians came on the way from Berlin to liberate Prague. They drove through and liberated the camp. And we all rushed out. It was in the evening, at night. And when I saw the business end of a tank, which was the first time I saw it, [SIGHS] I was just hoping they knew that we weren't Germans. But they did know. And they drove through. And lots of people just sat on the tanks and went to Prague. And I think I went a day later or two days later. My mother was working as a nurse for-- in the typhus hospital. So she didn't want to go. She wanted to look after her patients. So I went on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So in all those three years you were in Theresienstadt, was there a possibility each time that you would be sent with a transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. All the time. All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were living in total fear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Total fear all the time. You never--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You never knew when the day would come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because one day they'd take old people. And one day they'd take young people. And one day they took people whose name was from A to C. And the next-- you know, you never knew. There wasn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Unpredictable.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was unpredictable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you were always-- it always came at night. And you were always--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --living in fear that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you knew it was going to be much worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't know how bad. But you knew, as long as you were in Theresienstadt, you were on your home ground, practically.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we knew it's much colder in Poland and much more primitive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, we'll continue on the next tape. Thank you. Right.
